mnemonics: Structure: Single Type, Union: United Memory
keyPoints: A structure allocates separate memory for each of its members, allowing all to hold values simultaneously.
A union allocates a single shared memory space large enough for its largest member, so only one member can hold a value at a time.
Structures are used to group related data of different types, while unions are used for memory efficiency when only one piece of data is needed at a time.
commonMistakes: Assuming a union's members can all be accessed and hold valid values at the same time, like a structure.
Forgetting that writing to one member of a union overwrites the value of any other member, as they share memory.
